text: The surgeon begins the dissection for a right inguinal hernia, starting medially approximately six to eight centimeters above the hernia defect. The fenestrated bipolar is used to measure the opening, which spans about two centimeters once fully exposed.

The surgeon continues the dissection laterally, maintaining a horizontal approach, before moving medially into the retro-rectus space. The dissection progresses towards the symphysis pubis, with careful manipulation using a fenestrated bipolar near the bladder.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection using a fenestrated bipolar, angling it downward to catch the edge of the peritoneum and pull it downward. The medial and lateral dissections are brought together, converging at the hernia sac.

The surgeon works just above the hernia sac, reducing the indirect hernia defect. The area inferior to the hernia sac is approached with caution due to the presence of nerves and vessels, aided by clear visualization.

The surgeon completes the dissection in the inferior portion, extending one to two centimeters below the symphysis pubis and two centimeters across the midline. The mesh is positioned to cover this area, ensuring it is trapped when the bladder re-expands. The dissection is performed carefully over the femoral artery and vein to clear the space and prevent the peritoneum from sliding under the mesh.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the vessels and cord structures near the femoral canal, using scissors to gently pull away from the femoral vein. The mesh is positioned flat in the groove created, with the myopectineal orifice fully exposed for easy mesh placement.
